audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Ruth gate proceeding, land redemption, nearer redeemer, sandal witness, Ruth and Boaz marriage, birth, Naomi's caregiving, and Davidic genealogy narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for gate assembly, unnamed nearer redeemer, Naomi's land transaction, redemption disclosure, Ruth/Mahlon name-continuity clause, inheritance concern, sandal custom, witness formulas, Rachel/Leah/Tamar blessing, conception language, women's blessing of Naomi, goel child language, better-than-seven-sons praise, Naomi's caregiving role, neighborhood naming, Obed/Jesse/David link, and Perez genealogy.

verses.restored: Boaz went up to gate and sat there. Look, the redeemer of whom Boaz had spoken was passing by. He said, Turn aside, sit here, so-and-so. He turned aside and sat.
Boaz took ten men from elders of city and said, Sit here. They sat.
He said to redeemer, Naomi, who has returned from fields of Moab, is selling portion of field that belonged to our brother Elimelech.

I said, I will uncover your ear, saying, Acquire it before those sitting here and before elders of my people. If you will redeem, redeem. If you will not redeem, tell me, so I may know, because there is no one besides you to redeem, and I am after you. He said, I will redeem.
Boaz said, On day you acquire field from hand of Naomi, you also acquire Ruth the Moabite, wife of the dead, to raise name of dead upon his inheritance.
The redeemer said, I am not able to redeem it for myself, lest I ruin my inheritance. Redeem for yourself my right of redemption, because I am not able to redeem.
Now this was formerly in Israel concerning redemption and concerning exchange, to establish every matter: a man removed his sandal and gave it to his neighbor. This was the testimony in Israel.
The redeemer said to Boaz, Acquire it for yourself, and he removed his sandal.
Boaz said to elders and all people, You are witnesses today that I have acquired all that belonged to Elimelech and all that belonged to Chilion and Mahlon from hand of Naomi.

Also Ruth the Moabite, wife of Mahlon, I have acquired for myself as wife, to raise name of dead upon his inheritance, so name of dead may not be cut off from among his brothers and from gate of his place. You are witnesses today.

All people who were at gate and elders said, We are witnesses. May YHWH make woman who is coming into your house like Rachel and like Leah, who together built house of Israel. Do strength in Ephrathah, and proclaim a name in Bethlehem.
May your house be like house of Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah, from offspring that YHWH will give you from this young woman.
Boaz took Ruth, and she became his wife. He went in to her, and YHWH gave her conception, and she bore a son.
The women said to Naomi, Blessed be YHWH, who has not left you without a redeemer today. May his name be proclaimed in Israel.
May he become for you a restorer of life and a sustainer of your old age, because your daughter-in-law who loves you has borne him—she who is better to you than seven sons.
Naomi took the child, placed him in her bosom, and became his caregiver.
The neighboring women gave him a name, saying, A son has been born to Naomi. They called his name Obed. He was father of Jesse, father of David.
These are generations of Perez: Perez fathered Hezron;
Hezron fathered Ram; Ram fathered Amminadab;
Amminadab fathered Nahshon; Nahshon fathered Salmon;
Salmon fathered Boaz; Boaz fathered Obed;
Obed fathered Jesse, and Jesse fathered David.

translation_notes: The gate proceeding is public economic law involving land, kinship, inheritance, and household continuity. Public witness offers accountability, but women remain discussed within male-governed structures.
The nearer redeemer is left unnamed—'so-and-so' or an intentionally concealed name. His refusal concerns his inheritance; the text does not require inventing moral defects beyond what is stated.

Verses 5 and 10 use acquisition language for land and for Ruth's marriage within an ancient patriarchal legal system. Reporting that language must not make Ruth property or erase the necessity of consent.
Ruth remains 'the Moabite' even as she enters a Bethlehem household and genealogy. Belonging does not require ethnic erasure, xenophobia, or contempt for Moabites.
The duty to raise the dead man's name combines family continuity with property transmission. It is related to, but not a simple enactment of, the levirate law in Deuteronomy 25.
The sandal exchange is described as an older legal custom and public testimony. It is not humiliation of the unnamed redeemer and should not be reconstructed beyond the text.

The blessing invokes Rachel, Leah, and Tamar—women whose stories include reproductive struggle, exploitation, rivalry, and constrained agency. Their names must not become decorative validation of patriarchy.

YHWH gives conception in the narrative, but fertility is not proof of virtue, Divine favor, womanhood, or successful marriage. Infertility, pregnancy loss, childlessness, and nonparenthood are not spiritual failures.

The women call the child a redeemer for Naomi and praise Ruth as better than seven sons. This honors a foreign daughter-in-law above a conventional ideal of male offspring; it does not reduce Ruth to reproduction.
Naomi becomes the child's caregiver or foster-mother. Her restored community and purpose matter, but a baby does not erase bereavement or make older women valuable only through caregiving.
'A son has been born to Naomi' is communal kinship language, not denial that Ruth is the child's mother.

The genealogy connects the story to David. It must not erase Ruth and Naomi beneath a royal endpoint, authorize monarchy uncritically, or support Christian supersessionism over Jewish scripture and identity.
safety_note: Do not treat Ruth as purchased property, marriage as automatic consent, childbirth as women's purpose, or fertility as moral reward. Preserve Ruth's Moabite identity, Naomi's grief and caregiving dignity, public accountability, and the difference between ancient kinship law and modern ethical relationships.
